text.title: Legal Compliance — Sub-component Map
text.summary: Routing guide for the legal compliance domain: maps common founder situations to the correct sub-methodology.

section.title: Sub-components
section.p: Legal compliance for online businesses is split into two focused methodologies to keep each loadable and actionable on its own.

rule.statement: Load ops-legal-basics for foundation work (entity choice, policy drafting, IP, contracts). Load ops-legal-compliance-checklist for stage-by-stage implementation tracking and auditing.
rule.rationale: Splitting foundation knowledge from implementation tracking allows an agent to load only what the task requires, avoiding token waste.

rule.statement: Use this routing table to select the correct sub-methodology before starting any legal compliance task.
rule.rationale: Routing errors (e.g. going straight to the checklist without reading the policy framework) produce incomplete outputs.

list.item: Setting up business entity → ops-legal-basics (Step 1: structure)
Drafting Terms or Privacy policies → ops-legal-basics (Step 2: policies)
Contractor or customer agreements → ops-legal-basics (Step 5: contracts)
Website legal page audit → ops-legal-compliance-checklist (Website Requirements)
Policy update process → ops-legal-compliance-checklist (Policy Update Process)

rule.statement: Always mark any agent-generated policy text as draft requiring attorney review before publication.
rule.rationale: LLM-drafted Terms or Privacy policies may hallucinate legal references, omit jurisdiction-mandatory clauses, or import US terms into non-US contexts. Publishing without counsel review is malpractice exposure.

rule.statement: Every agent task on legal compliance must declare the jurisdiction set (e.g. US-only, US+EU, US+EU+UK) before generating any output.
rule.rationale: The same data flow can be lawful in one jurisdiction and unlawful in another. Undeclared jurisdiction produces silently wrong guidance.

antipattern.description: Agent auto-applies a single cookie-banner pattern across all markets.
antipattern.reason: EU requires opt-in; CCPA requires opt-out; Quebec Law 25 is hybrid. One pattern violates at least one regime.

antipattern.description: Agent auto-publishes policy changes without a redline + human review gate.
antipattern.reason: Silent policy edits hide changes from counsel, create litigation risk, and may violate user notification obligations.
